title: MTTR by Priority Trending
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-365d@d
| where lower(state) IN ("closed","resolved","6","7")
| eval open_ts=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval close_ts=strptime(closed_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval mttr_hr=round((close_ts-open_ts)/3600,2)
| where mttr_hr>=0 AND isnotnull(priority)
| eval _time=close_ts
| bin _time span=1q
| stats avg(mttr_hr) as mttr_hr by priority, _time
| sort _time priority
description: Priority-weighted resolution time shows whether urgent work truly gets faster attention; quarterly trends highlight chronic bottlenecks for P1/P2 paths.
value: Priority-weighted resolution time shows whether urgent work truly gets faster attention; quarterly trends highlight chronic bottlenecks for P1/P2 paths.
implementation: Confirm timestamp formats and business-pause fields; subtract `business_wait` or `on_hold_duration` if extracted. Normalize priority text (`1 - Critical` ‚Üí `P1`). Use `close_ts` as the event time so each resolution lands in the correct quarter. For a single ‚Äúall priorities‚Äù reference line, add a second panel with `stats avg(mttr_hr) by _time` without splitting by `priority`. Pair with staffing and major-incident dashboards when P1 MTTR regresses.
visualization: Line chart (MTTR hours by priority over quarters), Heatmap (priority √ó quarter), Optional overlay (overall MTTR from a companion search).
knownFalsePositives: MTTR and response drifts from harder incidents, new staff, on-call experience mix, or a wider scope of what we close as done.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_snow`.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:incident"` (`opened_at`, `closed_at`, `priority`); `sourcetype=jira:*` with `created`/`resolved`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Confirm timestamp formats and business-pause fields; subtract `business_wait` or `on_hold_duration` if extracted. Normalize priority text (`1 - Critical` ‚Üí `P1`). Use `close_ts` as the event time so each resolution lands in the correct quarter. For a single ‚Äúall priorities‚Äù reference line, add a second panel with `stats avg(mttr_hr) by _time` without splitting by `priority`. Pair with staffing and major-incident dashboards when P1 MTTR regresses.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" earliest=-365d@d
| where lower(state) IN ("closed","resolved","6","7")
| eval open_ts=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval close_ts=strptime(closed_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval mttr_hr=round((close_ts-open_ts)/3600,2)
| where mttr_hr>=0 AND isnotnull(priority)
| eval _time=close_ts
| bin _time span=1q
| stats avg(mttr_hr) as mttr_hr by priority, _time
| sort _time priority
```

Understanding this SPL

**MTTR by Priority Trending** ‚Äî Priority-weighted resolution time shows whether urgent work truly gets faster attention; quarterly trends highlight chronic bottlenecks for P1/P2 paths.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:incident"` (`opened_at`, `closed_at`, `priority`); `sourcetype=jira:*` with `created`/`resolved`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_snow`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:incident. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:incident", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where lower(state) IN ("closed","resolved","6","7")` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **open_ts** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **close_ts** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **mttr_hr** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where mttr_hr>=0 AND isnotnull(priority)` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **_time** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by priority, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Compare with the Jira issue page, board, or service project view for the same dates. Confirm issue keys, status, and assignment fields; reconcile timestamp fields you use in the report.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (MTTR hours by priority over quarters), Heatmap (priority √ó quarter), Optional overlay (overall MTTR from a companion search).
grandmaExplanation: We trend fix time by how urgent work is, so we see if our promises to customers are drifting over the quarters.
